HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF MARTA GREENBERG's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents And Production Of Documents, And Request For Monetary Sanctions.

Both sides stipulate to the matter being decided by the pro tem judge. The pro tem judge and counsel for both sides (all appearing remotely) authorize the clerk to sign the formal stipulation form on their behalf.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with a modification.

PLAINTIFF MARTA GREENBERG'S (HEREAFTER EITHER "DEFENDANT" OR "GREENBERG) MOTION TO COMPEL DEFENDANT DIGITAL MEDIA SOLUTIONS LLC (HEREAFTER EITHER "DEFENDANT" OR "DMS") TO FURTHER RESPOND TO PLAINTIIFF'S REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS NOS 4, 5, 11,12, 13 & 15 IS GRANTED AS FOLLOWS:

(1) DEFENDANTS OBJECTIONS TO RPD NOS 4, 5, 11, 12, 13, & 15 ARE OVERRULED.
(2) TO THE EXTENT THAT IT HAS NOT ALREADY DONE SO, DEFENDANT WILL DELIVER TO PLAINTIFFS ALL RESPONSIVE DOCUMENTS CALLED FOR BY RPD NOS 4, 5, 11, 12, 13 & 15. IF DEFENDANT IS WITHHOLDING ANY RESPONSIVE DOCUMENT CLAIMING THAT IT IS SUBJECT TO A CLAIM OF ATTORNEY/CLIENT PRIVILEGE, IT WILL IDENTIFY THE DOCUMENT WITH SUFFICIENT SPECIFICITY TO ALLOW PLAINTIFFS TO CHALLENGE THE A/C CLAIM.
(3) IF DEFENDANT'S RESPONSE TO ANY OF THE ABOVE NUUMBERED RPD IS THAT IT HAS NO SUCH DOCUMENTS IT WILL DELIVER TO PLAINTIFFS AN AFFIDAVIT DETAILING WHETHER AT ANY TIME IT HAD SUCH A DOCUMENT, WHY IT IS NO LONGER AVAILABLE AND DETAILING DMS' EFFORTS TO OBTAIN THE REQUESTED DOCUMENT.
(4) THE COURT WILL IMPOSE MONETARY SANCTIONS IN THE AMOUNT OF FIFTEEN HUNDRED ($1,500.00) DOLLARS AGAINST DEFENDANT AND ITS ATTORNEYS PAYABLE WITHIN TEN (10) CALENDAR DAYS FROM THE DATE OF THIS ORDER TO PLAINTIFFS AND THEIR ATTORNEYS.

COUNSEL FOR PLAINTIFFS ARE TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH IS TO INCLUDE THE MODIFIED TIMELINE DISCUSSED AT THE HEARING, SUBMIT A COPY TO DEFENDANT'S COUNSEL FOR APPROVAL AS TO FORM, AND DELIVER A COPY IN WORD FORM TO THE COURT AT rennepar85@gmail.com.

Second Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings, Or In The Alternative, To Strike (Motions)
Off Calendar May-23-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT DIGITAL MEDIA SOLUTIONS LLC'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings, Or In The Alternative, To Strike is denied.

Defendant's motion violates CCP sec. 438(g) because there has been no material change since the court overruled defendant's prior demurrer, which raised the same inapposite claim preclusion argument. The motion to strike is untimely because it should have been brought with the demurrer. (See CRC 3.1322(b).)

In any event, defendant's contention that "res judicata" or claim preclusion bars plaintiff's subject line claim under Business and Professions Code sec. 17529.5(a)(3) completely lacks merit. "Claim preclusion arises if a second suit involves: (1) the same cause of action (2) between the same parties (3) after a final judgment on the merits in the first suit." (DKN Holdings LLC v. Faerber (2015) 61 Cal.4th 813, 824.) In this case, claim preclusion does not apply because there is only one lawsuit. Moreover, there is no final judgment on the merits. The Court of Appeal ruled that: "[t]he judgment of dismissal is reversed, and the matter is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion." (Greenberg v. Digital Media Solutions, LLC (2021) 65 Cal.App.5th 909, 921(emphasis added).) Defendant's contention that the Court of Appeal affirmed "the judgment" as to the subject line claim is erroneous. The Court of Appeal affirmed the court's demurrer order, which concluded that the subject line claim was not cognizable under subsection (a)(2) of Business and Professions Code sec. 17529.5. An order sustaining a demurrer is distinct from a judgment. (See Berri v. Superior Court In and For City and County of San Francisco (1955) 43 Cal.2d 856 ["It is true that an order sustaining a demurrer without leave to amend is not appealable as it is not the final judgment in the case"].)

In its reply, defendant cites the legal principle that could theoretically apply to this action-the law of the case doctrine. Under the law of the case doctrine, when an appellate court " 'states in its opinion a principle or rule of law necessary to the decision, that principle or rule becomes the law of the case and must be adhered to throughout [the case's] subsequent progress, both in the lower court and upon subsequent appeal....' " (Kowis v. Howard (1992) 3 Cal.4th 888, 893.) Here, the Court of Appeal never ruled that plaintiff could not maintain an (a)(3) claim based on the subject line. Indeed, the court suggested the direct opposite. (See Greenberg, 65 Cal.App.5th at 916.) Therefore, this dilatory motion is denied in its entirety.

Order is signed.
